coredata.dc:title: In situ X-ray computational tomography measurement of single particle behavior of sulfide solid electrolyte under high-pressure compression
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kodama, M.
Ohashi, A.
Hirai, S.
coredata.dc:description: High-pressure in situ X-ray computational tomography measurements of sulfide solid electrolyte were conducted to elucidate the behavior of a single particle. Chlorine in Li6PS5Cl solid electrolyte was replaced by iodine to obtain high-contrast X-ray computational tomography measurements, and the behavior of a single particle under high pressure was successfully visualized. The experimental results were statistically analyzed to extract the generalized behavior of a solid electrolyte particle. The results show that pressurization increased the contact area and sphericity of a solid electrolyte particle and suppressed the voids between and within the solid electrolyte particles, thereby enhancing ionic conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-solid-state battery
Sulfide solid electrolyte
In situ measurement
X-ray computational tomography
Single particle measurement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray computational tomography images (a) without and (b) with an imaging solid electrolyte particle. The applied pressure was 10 MPa.
(a) Stress–strain characteristics of solid electrolytes comprising volume fractions of Li6PS5Cl:Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 of 100:0, 50:50, and 0:100. (b) XRD pattern of Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5.

(a) Increase in ratio of contact area to applied pressure (dR
c /dp) as a function of initial contact ratio (R
c ). Particle X-ray computational tomography three-dimensional images (topside) and two-dimensional CT raw images (bottom side) of (b) low and (c) high initial contact ratio particles (yellow: non-contact area; purple: contact area). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Increase in ratio of sphericity to applied pressure (dS
p /dp) as a function of initial sphericity (S
p ). Three-dimensional images and two-dimensional images of X-ray computational tomography of particles with (b) low and (c) high initial sphericity (left-side images show surface rendering; middle images show sliced depictions; right-side images show two-dimensional raw CT image).
Correlation coefficients of influence of pressure on sphericity and contact ratio.

head.ce:title: In situ X-ray computational tomography measurement of single particle behavior of sulfide solid electrolyte under high-pressure compression

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tokyo Institute of Technology, NE-8 2-12-1, Meguro, Tokyo, 152-8550, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tokyo Institute of Technology, NE-8 2-12-1, Meguro, Tokyo, 152-8550, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-pressure in situ X-ray computational tomography measurements of sulfide solid electrolyte were conducted to elucidate the behavior of a single particle. Chlorine in Li6PS5Cl solid electrolyte was replaced by iodine to obtain high-contrast X-ray computational tomography measurements, and the behavior of a single particle under high pressure was successfully visualized. The experimental results were statistically analyzed to extract the generalized behavior of a solid electrolyte particle. The results show that pressurization increased the contact area and sphericity of a solid electrolyte particle and suppressed the voids between and within the solid electrolyte particles, thereby enhancing ionic conductivity.

ce:section.ce:para: The all-solid-state lithium-ion battery (ASSLiB) is a prospective candidate for use as a next-generation electric vehicle battery. In ASSLiBs, the combustible liquid electrolyte used in conventional lithium-ion batteries is replaced by a solid electrolyte, which, owing to its incombustibility and chemical stability, enables the battery to operate safely across a wide range of temperatures [1,2]. However, practical application of ASSLiBs requires an increase in energy density and charging speed. For a high energy density and high power output ASSLiB, ionic conduction through the solid electrolyte should be smooth and with low voltage loss.

Various solid electrolytes with high ionic conductivity have been developed [3]. Sulfide solid electrolyte has a high lithium ionic conductivity: Li-argyrodite, Li6PS5X (X = Cl, Br, I), has a high ionic conductivity (1.3 mS/cm) with high electrochemical stability [4]; Li9.54Si1.74P1.44S11.7Cl0.3 has achieved the highest ionic conductivity of 25 mS/cm at room temperature [5]. These sulfide solid electrolytes can achieve high ionic conductivity with high-pressure compression (>100 MPa). High-pressure compression reduces the voids between the solid electrolyte particles, thereby forming a lithium-ion conduction path and enabling high ionic conductivity to be achieved. It was revealed from in situ high-pressure X-ray computational tomography (X-ray CT) that the lithium-ion conduction pass is determined only by the three-dimensional (3D) distribution of solid electrolyte and that the contact resistance between solid electrolyte particles is negligibly small [6]. However, the mechanism a single particle affects the reduction of voids in a solid electrolyte powder under pressure has not been clarified. Reduction of voids can be caused by various phenomena, such as the crushing of individual particles, plastic deformation, and interaction between particles. There have not been any reports concerning experimental measurement of behavior of a solid electrolyte particle under high-pressure compression. In numerical simulation, the particle behaviors of solid electrolyte were simulated by Giménez et al. using the discrete-element method (DEM) [7]. In this DEM simulation, the shapes of the particles in an ASSLiB electrode were assumed to be spherical, and the ionic and electronic conductions were calculated by the length, width, and 3D distribution of the conduction pass. For a more realistic numerical simulation with DEM, the solid electrolyte should not be treated as a sphere and experimental validation of single particle behavior under high-pressure compression is necessary.

From the background mentioned above, in this study, we developed a solid electrolyte single particle observation method with in-situ X-ray CT measurement and elucidated the particle behavior of a solid electrolyte under high-pressure compression.

The X-ray CT measurement was conducted using the same system and cell as described in a previous study [6]. The applied pressure was changed from 0 MPa to 100 MPa and ionic conductivity increased with pressure. Fig. 1 (a) illustrates X-ray CT images of compressed Li6PS5Cl. As shown, it was difficult to identify a single particle and, moreover, to extract the 3D shape and contact condition with adjacent particles. To analyze and discuss particle behavior, a single particle must be clearly distinguishable from others. Therefore, in this study, we prepared an imaging solid electrolyte by replacing 50% of the chlorine in Li6PS5Cl with iodine to obtain Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5. Iodine has a higher X-ray attenuation coefficient, so the prepared L6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 could be clearly distinguished from neighboring particles. The Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 were made by mechanical milling using starting materials of Li2S, P2S5, LiCl, and LiI, followed by heat treatment, in the same manner as described in our previous study [6]. With the laboratory X-ray CT system used in this study, the resolution of 1.0 μm was insufficient to measure some microstructures, so large solid electrolyte particles, ranging in size from 75 μm to 150 μm, were specifically selected to prevent formation of small-scale voids that would be smaller than the resolution of the X-ray CT system. The imaging particles (Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5) were mixed into the Li6PS5Cl at a mass fraction of 1%. Fig. 1(b) shows X-ray CT images of Li6PS5Cl with the Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 solid electrolyte. A single particle of L6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 can clearly be distinguished by its white color. With regard to the possibility of a change in mechanical properties with the replacement of chlorine by iodine, we checked the powder-mechanical characteristics with various volume fraction ratios of Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5. Fig. 2 (a) illustrates stress–strain characteristics with volume fractions of Li6PS5Cl:Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 of 100:0, 50:50, and 0:100. The stress-strain characteristics are measured by same method use in the previous study [6], and the stress is measured by the strain of the spring in the cell and the strain is obtained by measuring the distance between the current correctors. The stress–strain characteristics were almost the same in all three cases; therefore, the mechanical characteristics of Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 particles were almost the same. This tendency is coincident with chloride or iodine doped 75Li2S–25P2S5 solid electrolyte [8]. Fig. 2 (b) illustrates the XRD pattern of Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5. The XRD pattern originates from Li6PS5Cl and Li6PS5I, and Li6PS5Cl0.5I0.5 is a mixed structure. The ionic conductivity is 8.8×10-5 [S/cm] at room temperature and is much smaller than that of Li6PS5Cl (1.3×10-3 [S/cm]), this can be attributed to suppression of ion transportation by iodine anions [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle characteristic calculation and statistical processing
ce:section.ce:para:
The single particle 3D shape and contact condition with adjacent particles were evaluated using the parameters of sphericity (S
p ) and contact ratio (R
c ). S
p is a parameter used to describe particle shape in a powder, and is defined as the ratio of the surface area of a particle to the surface area of a sphere with the same volume as the particle (Eq. (1)) [10,11]. The S
p value decreases with an increase in surface irregularities and with an increase in voids inside the particle. If the voids within a particle decrease, the tortuosity within the particle decreases, and its effective ionic conductivity increases. R
c is defined as the ratio of total surface area of a particle (S) to its contact surface area with adjacent particles (S
c ), as shown by Eq. (2). From our previous study, the contact area is equal to the cross-sectional area of a lithium-ion conduction pass, so the contact ratio represents the ion transportation ability between the solid electrolyte particles [6].
(1)
S p
=
π
(
6 ∗ V
π
)
2 3
S
(2)
R c
=
S c
S

Generalized results cannot be obtained from one measurement of single particle observation because single particle measurements include specific phenomena that pertain to each particle (initial shape, initial state, applied pressure). Therefore, in this study, 160 X-ray CT images obtained for 40 particles with varying applied pressures were statistically analyzed. The values of four initial parameters before compression (initial R
c ; initial S
p ; initial volume; initial surface area) and two parameters relating to the particle behavior on compression (rate of increase of contact ratio to applied pressure, dR
c /dp; rate of increase in ratio of sphericity to applied pressure, dS
p /dp) were calculated and used for the analysis. The initial parameters and those obtained from the pressure derivatives were calculated for each particle, so the total number of values for each parameter was 40. The statistical analysis was conducted by using HAD statistical analysis software using a correlation analysis [12]. The correlation coefficient (r) of parameter x and parameter y for n data points was calculated by Eq. (3); the p-value for the correlation coefficient, which shows the accuracy of the r value, was also calculated. A p-value of less than 0.05 indicates that there is an effective correlation. Positive and negative values of r indicate the direction of the correlation between x and y (if r is positive, the correlation between x and y is positive, and vice versa). The absolute value of r shows the intensity of the correlation: 1 ≥ |r| ≥ 0.7 indicates high correlation, 0.7 > |r| ≥ 0.4 is a moderate correlation, 0.4 > |r| ≥ 0.2 is a low correlation, and 0.2 > |r| ≥ 0.0 shows an almost negligible relationship.
(3)
r =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
x i
−
x ¯
)
(
y i
−
y ¯
)
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
x i
−
x ¯
)
2
×
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
y i
−
y ¯
)
2

Table 1 shows the correlation coefficients between the initial shape parameters (R
c , S
p , volume, surface area) calculated before compression and several parameters (initial R
c , initial S
p , dR
c /dp, dS
p /dp). Here, dR
c /dp and dS
p /dp were calculated by the increasing ratio of R
c and S
p with pressurization for the same particle. Table 1 shows that three correlations (initial surface area–initial S
p , dR
c /dp–initial R
c , and dS
p /dp–initial S
p ) had low p-values. There were no correlations between the initial R
c and initial volume or initial surface area. The correlation between initial surface area and initial S
p originates from the definition of S
p shown in Eq (2). Therefore, in this study, we focused on dR
c /dp–initial R
c and dS
p /dp–initial S
p to describe the behavior of a solid electrolyte particle under high-pressure compression.

Fig. 3 (a) shows dR
c /dp as a function of initial R
c . dR
c /dp decreased with the increase in R
c
, and gave a positive value with low initial R
c (initial R
c < 30%) and the average of that was almost zero when the initial R
c was high (initial R
c > 30%). Therefore, these results indicate that if the initial contact ratio is low, the contact ratio increases with an increase in the applied pressure, but, if the initial contact ratio is high, the contact ratio does not increase with an increase in the applied pressure.

Fig. 3(b) and (c) show 3D X-ray CT images and raw CT images of solid electrolyte particles at (b) low and (c) high initial contact ratios. The contact areas with adjacent particles are shown in purple. In the case of a low initial contact ratio (Fig. 3(b)), the contact area expanded with the increase in pressure; in contrast, with a high initial contact ratio (Fig. 3(c)), expansion of the contact surface with pressurization was not significant and there was some movement of the contact surface. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the strain of solid electrolyte increased by 20% under stress from 15 MPa to 95 MPa. However, strain of the particle cannot be seen in the X-ray CT images shown in Fig. 3. This is because the strain of the power is induced mainly by the void reduction between solid electrolyte particles.

Ion transportation resistance between solid electrolyte particles originates from the contact area between the particles; contact resistance in the contact area is negligibly small [6]. This result suggests that ion transportation between solid electrolyte particles mainly depends on the initial contact ratio and applied pressure; therefore, if high ionic conductivity of solid electrolyte is necessary under low applied pressure, the initial contact ratio should be improved. The initial contact ratio cannot be controlled by the particle size, as shown by the data in Table 1; thus, to achieve high ionic conductivity in a solid electrolyte with low applied pressure, the particle arrangement should be controlled. Shaking of a powder can control the arrangement of particles [13], so it is assumed that shaking the solid electrolyte before the compression may improve the ionic conductivity under low pressure.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the increase in the ratio of sphericity to applied pressure (dS
p /dp) as a function of initial sphericity (S
p ). dS
p /dp monotonically decreased with an increase in initial S
p and took a positive value for low initial S
p (initial S
p < 0.45) and a negative value for high initial S
p (initial S
p > 0.45). Therefore, this result indicates that if the initial sphericity is low, the sphericity is increased with an increase in applied pressure; however, if the initial sphericity is high, the sphericity is decreased.

Fig. 4(b) and (c) show 3D X-ray CT images of particles and 2D raw CT image with (b) low and (c) high initial sphericity, where images on the left side are those with rendered surface and those on the middle are sliced images, and those on the right side are the raw CT images. For the case with low initial sphericity particle (Fig. 4 (b)), many small voids are present within a particle under low applied pressure. These voids increased the surface area of the solid electrolyte and the sphericity was low. With an increase in applied pressure, the voids collapsed, no internal voids were present, and sphericity increased. This decrease of the internal voids contributes to an increase in the ionic conduction path and leads to an increase in the ionic conductivity of this particle. In the case of the particle with high initial sphericity (Fig. 4(c)), there were no internal voids, even at low applied pressure. With an increase in applied pressure, cracks and hollows were generated on the particle surface due to plastic deformation caused by contact with adjacent particles. These cracks and hollows increased the sphericity, but sphericity decreased with the increase in applied pressure.

The results concerning the different changes of sphericity with differing initial sphericity (initial internal voids) suggest that to achieve a high-accuracy DEM simulation of sulfide solid electrolyte, the effect of initial particle shape should be considered; particles should not simply be assumed to be perfect spheres. Effects associated with the interactions between particles cannot be accounted for by single particle measurements as conducted in this study. However, with the use of findings and morphological information provided in this study, highly accurate DEM simulation can be conducted to elucidate the relationship between morphology and ionic conductivity.

From the discussion of contact ratio and sphericity, it is proposed that the increase of the ionic conductivity of sulfide solid electrolyte with void suppression originates from two phenomena: one is the increase of contact area of a particle with low initial contact area, which enhances ionic conduction between solid electrolyte particles; the other is the increase of ionic conduction in a solid electrolyte particle with suppression of the internal voids, which occurs on collapse of the solid electrolyte particles.

Three-dimensional high-pressure in situ X-ray CT measurements were conducted to elucidate the single particle behavior of sulfide solid electrolyte. The following conclusions can be drawn.
1.
Replacement of chlorine by iodine does not significantly change the mechanical characteristics of the sulfide solid electrolyte, and the single particle behavior in compressed sulfide solid electrolyte was successfully observed with X-ray CT.
2.
For particles with a low initial contact ratio, the contact ratio increased with pressurization, but the contact ratio of particles with a high initial contact ratio did not increase. The initial contact ratio does not depend on the initial particle shape characteristics (particle volume, surface area, and sphericity), but depends on the initial particle arrangement before compression.
3.
In the case of particles with low initial sphericity, sphericity increased with pressurization as the voids inside the solid electrolyte particles collapsed. In contrast, a particle with a high initial sphericity decreased its sphericity with pressurization, owing to plastic deformation caused by contact with surrounding particles.
4.
The increase of ionic conductivity of sulfide solid electrolyte by high-pressure compression with a decrease of voids is caused by an increase in the contact area between the solid electrolyte particles and by reduction of voids in the particles.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Japan Keirin Autorace Foundation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding the origin of enhanced Li-ion transport in nanocrystalline argyrodite-type Li6PS5I
Chem. Mater.
